channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Evil Hat Co-Founder Talks About Potential End of the Fate System
item.content:encoded:
Evil Hat Productions' flagship TTRPG system might be nearing its end. The Fate system, a TTRPG system that caught many players' attention in the 2010s, has dwindled so much in sales, the co-founder of the company has voiced the possibility that its next project may be the last.

Evil Hat Productions and The Possible End of the Fate System

The potential end of Evil Hat Productions' Fate system was expressed in an in-depth interview with company co-founder Fred Hicks by Rascal News. The interview overall covered the origins of the Fate system, its inspirations, its popularity, and its current state.

Briefly, the Fate system debuted for free in 2003 and started as a hack for a different open-source TTRPG called Fudge. The system highly emphasized character and story fiction over simulation. One of the key mechanics were Aspects, phrases or words that represented key parts of a character. Whenever dice were rolled, Aspects could be tagged for bonuses, or penalties, to the result, leading to more thematically impactful conflicts.

This version of the game would then be refined into the Fate Core system, which would go on to be a crowdfunding success in 2013, raising over $400,000. This success lead to several years of strong ongoing support with a library of micro-settings, titled Fate Worlds of Adventure, different supplements and alternative rules systems, and the acclaimed Dresden Files RPG, adapting Jim Butcher's iconic urban fantasy novel series.

In the 2010s, Fate's influence began to wane. The system had it's first "edition war" between fans of the original Fate Core and the revised Fate Accelerated system. Book sales favored GM resources like the Horror and Space Toolkits over settings, but such books take a lot of time to develop. Then there was the mixed critical reception to 2020's Fate of Cthulhu, which further hurt the system's appeal.

As the Fate system stands now in the 2020s, it is comparatively bleak. Fate products account for less than 5% of the company's sales. Because of this fact, the next major release, Umdaar is in a precarious place. 

The book is arguably Fate's largest original setting, a 400-page sword and planet sci-fi fantasy world heavily inspired by Masters of the Universe. However, it will not be getting a retail printing, but instead be available in PDF format and POD. The reasoning being that the setting and system are being aimed at too niche of a market. The volatile global market caused by unwelcome conflicts and trade tariffs are also cited.

While this reads as doom for Evil Hat Productions, they're still doing well as a TTRPG publisher. They have a diverse catalog of titles with active support and passionate fandoms like Apocalypse Keys, Thirsty Sword Lesbians, Blades in the Dark, and Monster of the Week. As for the Fate system itself, it is open source, allowing any aspiring designers to use the system as a template for their own future projects.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/evil-hat-co-founder-talks-about-potential-end-of-fate-system
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
